hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Flaccum' — 'Flaccus' with -us is nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative of 2nd declension -us nouns is 'Flacce' — this is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Flacci' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Flacci' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Flacci' ends in -i

steps.prompt: What do the abbreviations 'L' and 'C' stand for?

hints.Lucius and Gnaeus: 'Gn.' or 'Cn.' abbreviates Gnaeus — 'C.' stands for Gaius
hints.Largus and Gaius: 'Largus' is not a standard Roman praenomen abbreviation
hints.Laevus and Cornelius: Neither 'Laevus' nor 'Cornelius' matches the standard praenomen abbreviations L. and C.

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'Pomptinum' — 'Pomptinus' with -us is nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'Pomptine' — this is nominative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'Pomptini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'Pomptini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'Pomptini' ends in -i

hints.accusative: 'Praetores' can be accusative plural, but here it is in apposition with the nominative subjects
hints.vocative: Vocative is identical in form, but these are not being addressed — they are the subject
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'praetoribus' — 'praetores' is nominative or accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'praetoris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'praetoris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'praetoris' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What grammatical relationship does 'praetores' have to 'Flaccus et Pomptinus'?

hints.predicate nominative: A predicate nominative requires a linking verb — there is none here; 'praetores' is in apposition
hints.vocative address: Vocative would be direct address — 'praetores' describes the subjects in apposition
hints.subject complement with linking verb: No linking verb is present — 'praetores' is simply in apposition to the proper names

steps.prompt: 'Fortissimi' is what form of the adjective 'fortis'?
steps.reveal: fortissimi = very brave / bravest (nom. masc. pl.)

hints.comparative (genitive singular): Comparative of 'fortis' is 'fortior' — 'fortissimi' has the superlative -issim- suffix
hints.positive (nominative masculine plural): Positive nom. masc. pl. would be 'fortes' — 'fortissimi' has the -issim- superlative marker
hints.superlative (genitive masculine singular: Genitive singular superlative would also be 'fortissimi,' but here it agrees with plural 'praetores'

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'et' in this phrase?

hints.adverb meaning 'also': 'Et' can mean 'also,' but here it clearly joins 'Flaccus' and 'Pomptinus'
hints.correlative with 'et...et': 'Et...et' means 'both...and' — here only one 'et' appears, simply coordinating
hints.introducing a result clause: 'Et' does not introduce result clauses — 'ut' does

translation: Lucius Flaccus and Gaius Pomptinus, most brave praetors
